title: OpenMic: A Multi-Agent-Based Stand-Up Comedy Generation System
research_questions: Can an end-to-end multi-agent system transform a user-provided life topic into a 3–5 minute Chinese stand-up performance and narrated comedy video?
Can retrieval-augmented generation and a fine-tuned JokeWriter mitigate the mismatch between Chinese humor datasets and long-form stand-up generation?
Can specialized agents improve comedic structure, timing, performability, and quality control through multi-round iterative refinement?
How do different sampling temperature settings affect LLM-as-a-judge scores for generated Chinese talk-show scripts?

methods_proposed.name: OpenMic
methods_proposed.summary: An end-to-end AutoGen-based multi-agent system that turns a user topic into a 3–5 minute Chinese stand-up performance and then into a narrated comedy video. It decomposes the pipeline into audience analysis, planning, script drafting, performance coaching, and quality control.

methods_proposed.name: Blackboard-centric multi-agent coordination
methods_proposed.summary: A shared blackboard stores audience profiles, topic expansions, draft scripts, performance markup, and critiques so that agents read and write structured state instead of relying only on conversational context.

methods_proposed.name: Hierarchical multi-agent RAG with Secret Blackboard
methods_proposed.summary: A triadic inner-conversation architecture performs semantic retrieval, LLM candidate scoring, and LLM punchline refinement over an integrated comedic corpus. A Secret Blackboard hides noisy raw retrieval material from the main workflow and releases only refined writing materials to the JokeWriter.

methods_proposed.name: Dual-dimension QualityController
methods_proposed.summary: The QualityController separately evaluates retrieval quality and writer quality, then routes failures to re-retrieval, rewriting, or both. Termination occurs when both dimensions pass or the maximum iteration budget is reached.

methods_proposed.name: Comedy-specialized QLoRA JokeWriter
methods_proposed.summary: The paper fine-tunes a JokeWriter with QLoRA and completion-only loss to better capture setup–punchline delay, callbacks, and spoken-stage style.

methods_proposed.name: Structured performance DSL and video synthesis pipeline
methods_proposed.summary: The PerformanceCoach adds markers such as pauses, emphasis, pace changes, applause, and laughter cues. A downstream middleware parses these markers to synchronize synthesized audio and avatar performance in a narrated comedy video.

humor_theories.detail: The paper frames humor as controlled mismatch followed by coherent reinterpretation, and uses this view to motivate planning, verification, setup–punchline delay, hidden bridges, callbacks, and timing-aware delivery.
The paper introduces backdoor-style and frontdoor-style multi-step reasoning patterns, with a bridging entity E_Z that must be searched for and revealed at the right moment.

evaluation_methods.detail: A senior executive producer persona powered by `Grok-4-1-fast-reasoning` scores generated talk-show scripts with a Pydantic-enforced schema across Persona Fidelity, Humor Mechanics, Interactive Reactivity, Contextual Coherence, and Narrative Arc.
The paper defines a weighted total score as S_total = 0.30P + 0.25H + 0.20R + 0.15C + 0.10N, where P, H, R, C, and N are Persona, Humor, Reactivity, Coherence, and Narrative scores.

headline_results.finding: Under the temperature sweep, JW+Tem0.1 scored strongly on all five reported dimensions.
JW+Tem0.3 had high Persona, Humor, Narrative, and Coherence scores but very low Reactivity.
JW+Tem0.5 achieved the highest Humor score shown in Table 1 and the highest Coherence score shown in Table 1.
JW+Tem0.7 achieved the highest Persona and Narrative scores shown in Table 1, while Reactivity was lower than at T = 0.1.
In the multi-round refinement loop, the paper reports rapid convergence of the hardest failure case.

key_findings: The paper concludes that low temperature settings such as T = 0.1 combined with a large RAG-retrieved joke corpus yield the most superior results by preserving focus on retrieved comedic building blocks.

Higher temperatures such as T ≥ 0.7 can improve variety and Narrative Arc, with Narrative Arc reaching 98.5 at T = 0.7, but the paper says they often weaken Reactivity by causing the JokeWriter to ignore RAG-provided setups.
The triadic RAG workflow is designed to prioritize comedic potential rather than simple factual similarity by adding LLM-based candidate scoring and punchline refinement after semantic retrieval.
The blackboard and Secret Blackboard mechanisms are presented as ways to preserve structured state while keeping noisy retrieval material out of downstream agents’ context windows.
The PerformanceCoach’s DSL markers are used to bridge text generation and video rendering by specifying pauses, emphasis, pace changes, applause, and laughter cues.
The paper states that Case 3 failures in dual-dimension quality assessment drop from ~30% of round-1 attempts to <5% by round 3.
limitations: The paper states that humor evaluation is intrinsically difficult because funniness is subjective, culturally grounded, context-dependent, and sensitive to delivery and timing.
The paper states that commonly used Chinese humor datasets are often better suited for humor understanding and evaluation than for long-form stand-up generation.
The paper states that initial metrics in the temperature analysis exhibited a high degree of variance.
The paper reports LLM-as-a-judge evaluation but does not report human-audience or live-audience evaluation.
safety_ethics_notes: The AudienceAnalyzer produces a taboo/avoid list covering sensitive references and overly offensive wording, and the QualityController checks safety and audience fit as part of writer-quality assessment.

human_samples.population: Creators represented in pre-existing Chinese comedic source materials, including CFUN short-form setups/punchlines, traditional crosstalk scripts, and an LLM-processed Talkshow dataset.
human_samples.recruitment: Pre-existing corpora; no newly recruited human participants or creators are described.
human_samples.observations: Source corpus items/scripts are used for retrieval and fine-tuning; corpus sizes and creator counts are not reported.
human_samples.detail: The paper uses existing comedic materials but reports no human evaluation participants, annotators, or live-audience study.

judges.name: Grok-4-1-fast-reasoning
judges.detail: Used as a senior executive producer persona for LLM-as-a-judge scoring of generated talk-show scripts across Persona Fidelity, Humor Mechanics, Interactive Reactivity, Contextual Coherence, and Narrative Arc; this determines Table 1/Figure 6 temperature-evaluation results.

judges.name: QualityController; underlying model not reported
judges.detail: Acts as critic/gatekeeper in OpenMic, evaluating coherence, comedic payoff, timing realism, audience fit, retrieval quality, and writer quality; outputs PASS or REVISION and supplies the Q_r^R/Q_r^W labels underlying the reported Case 3 convergence claim.

judges.name: LLM Candidate Scorer; underlying model not reported
judges.detail: Scores retrieved candidates for comedic potential and relevance, selecting high-potency jokes/materials for RAG; this affects generation inputs rather than an external evaluation benchmark.

judges.name: LLM Punchline Selector; underlying model not reported
judges.detail: Refines/distills selected RAG candidates into writing materials passed to JokeWriter.

judges.name: Weighted score S_total
judges.detail: The paper defines S_total = 0.30P + 0.25H + 0.20R + 0.15C + 0.10N over LLM-judge dimension scores, although Table 1 reports the five dimensions rather than total scores.
Section 5.2 reports qualitative review suggesting higher temperatures lead JokeWriter to ignore RAG-provided setups; no separate review protocol or reviewer count is reported.

generation_selection.scope: Temperature sweep for JokeWriter configurations JW+Tem0.1, JW+Tem0.3, JW+Tem0.5, JW+Tem0.7, and JW+Tem0.9
generation_selection.detail: The paper reports LLM-judge scores but does not state the number of prompts, generated scripts, repeated runs, or whether outputs were single samples, averages, or selected from multiple candidates. No best-of-N generation procedure is stated.

generation_selection.scope: OpenMic multi-round refinement workflow and end-to-end demo
generation_selection.detail: The workflow iteratively revises one evolving script based on QualityController feedback until PASS or a maximum round budget; the demo states the system was run for 3 iterations. This is iterative refinement, not best-of-N selection among independent sampled outputs. R_max is not reported.

generation_selection.scope: Qualitative single-agent and comparison examples in Figures 7-10
generation_selection.detail: The paper shows qualitative examples from GPT, DeepSeek, a QLoRA-finetuned model, and two temperature settings, but does not describe how examples were sampled or selected.

generation_selection.scope: Downstream video synthesis from a performance-ready script
generation_selection.detail: The paper describes rendering structured DSL-marked scripts into narrated comedy video via external digital-human APIs; no number of rendered candidates or selection procedure is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-5.2
model_provenance.detail: Mentioned as a strong general model in the preliminary qualitative comparison; Figure 7 shows a GPT-style generated response, but no API snapshot, access date, or decoding settings are reported.

model_provenance.name: DeepSeek
model_provenance.detail: Used in a preliminary qualitative comparison and Figure 8; exact DeepSeek model/version is not reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen
model_provenance.detail: Reported as the base model for comedy-specialized QLoRA fine-tuning in Table 2; fine-tuning was conducted on a single GPU and the final model was deployed via a vLLM entrypoint with LoRA support.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3-4B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Figure 9 is captioned 'QLora finetuned Qwen3-4B-Instruct', which conflicts with Table 2's Qwen-2.5-3B-Instruct base model; no separate implementation details are provided.

model_provenance.name: Grok-4-1-fast-reasoning
model_provenance.detail: Used as the LLM-as-a-judge model; no snapshot, inference date, access date, or judge decoding settings are reported.

model_provenance.name: Unspecified LLM(s) powering OpenMic core AutoGen agents
model_provenance.detail: AudienceAnalyzer, ComedyDirector, JokeWriter, PerformanceCoach, and QualityController are implemented as ConversableAgents, but their underlying model(s), versions, access mode, and inference dates are not specified.

model_provenance.name: Unspecified LLM(s) for RAG candidate scoring and punchline refinement
model_provenance.detail: The triadic RAG workflow uses an LLM Candidate Scorer and LLM Punchline Selector, but does not name the model(s).

model_provenance.name: Unspecified LLM for crosstalk-to-talkshow corpus processing
model_provenance.detail: Traditional crosstalk scripts were pushed through an LLM-driven refinement/anonymization/stylistic-conversion stage; the model is not identified.

model_provenance.name: Unspecified embedding model/function E
model_provenance.detail: The RAG retriever uses embeddings and cosine similarity, but the embedding model, index, and retrieval implementation are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Kling AI or other high-fidelity digital-human platform
model_provenance.detail: The video-synthesis section says the system invokes RESTful APIs from high-fidelity digital-human platforms, e.g. Kling AI; the exact platform/model/version actually used is not unambiguously specified.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Temperature values reported: 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9. Other sampling parameters are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: No number of prompts, generations per temperature, repetitions, random seeds, or averaging protocol is reported.
Scoring uses a Pydantic-enforced schema with five dimensions and a weighted total formula; the number of judge calls, judge prompt, temperature, token limits, and retry policy are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: RAG retrieval, scoring, and punchline refinement
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Semantic retrieval plus LLM Candidate Scorer plus LLM Punchline Selector per query are described, but exact call counts are not reported.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: top-k1 raw candidates are retrieved and a top-k2/thresholded subset is selected; k1, k2, and tau are not reported.
inference_budgets.token_budget: The paper says thousands of tokens of raw material are processed in the Secret Blackboard, but gives no exact token limits or context-window budgets.
inference_budgets.detail: RAG uses cosine similarity over an integrated comedic corpus, followed by LLM scoring and refinement; corpus size and retrieval settings are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Five core agents are scheduled across workflow rounds, with optional re-retrieval or rewriting; exact LLM-call counts per round are not reported.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: One evolving draft is revised; the demo reports 3 iterations. Maximum iteration budget R_max is referenced but not specified.
inference_budgets.detail: Termination occurs when both retrieval and writer dimensions pass or when the maximum round budget is reached.

inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Fine-tuning was conducted on a single GPU; final model deployed via vLLM with LoRA support. Inference compute and cost are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Reported training configuration includes 4-bit NF4 quantization, LoRA rank 16, alpha 32, dropout 0.05, learning rate 2e-4, paged AdamW 32-bit, per-device batch size 2, gradient accumulation 4, 1 epoch, and BF16 or FP16 precision.

The paper describes RESTful API calls to digital-human platforms and middleware parsing DSL markers, but does not report API call counts, rendering settings, cost, latency, or number of generated videos.

human_baselines.description: No human-written baseline scripts, human performers, live-audience ratings, or human evaluator scores are reported. Comparisons are qualitative model examples and LLM-judge scores.
human_baselines.detail: Because no human baseline is present, there are no materially matched human-vs-model conditions.

contamination_risk.basis: The paper does not evaluate on a standard public benchmark; it mainly judges generated outputs from system prompts. However, the retrieval and fine-tuning sources include public or pre-existing comedic corpora, the evaluation prompts and corpus/test separation are not specified, and there is no held-out split, decontamination test, or discussion of overlap between retrieved/training materials and evaluated outputs. Benchmark contamination is therefore not directly assessable, but leakage/originality risk is unclear.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Temperature-sweep scores and the conclusion that low temperature with RAG gives superior results; claims about higher-temperature effects on reactivity/narrative arc; QualityController-based iterative convergence claims such as Case 3 dropping from about 30% in round 1 to under 5% by round 3.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The main quantitative evidence comes from Grok-4-1-fast-reasoning as LLM-as-a-judge, and the refinement-loop evidence depends on LLM agent judgments. No human-audience or expert validation is provided.

reporting_gaps: No human evaluation, live-audience evaluation, or human baseline is reported.
No number of prompts, generated scripts, runs, repetitions, seeds, or averaging procedure is reported for Table 1 despite fractional scores and mention of high variance.
The exact prompts and sampled outputs underlying the temperature-sweep scores are not fully reported.
No judge prompt, judge decoding parameters, judge-call count, calibration, inter-rater reliability, or human validation is reported for the Grok LLM-as-a-judge setup.
No API snapshots, inference/access dates, or exact versions are reported for GPT-5.2, DeepSeek, Grok-4-1-fast-reasoning, the OpenMic agents, the embedding model, or video-generation services.
The underlying LLM(s) for AudienceAnalyzer, ComedyDirector, JokeWriter during inference, PerformanceCoach, QualityController, LLM Candidate Scorer, and LLM Punchline Selector are not specified.
RAG corpus sizes, item counts, source splits, k1, k2, threshold tau, embedding model, index configuration, and retrieval hyperparameters are not reported.
The LLM used to process/anonymize/convert traditional crosstalk scripts into talk-show style is not reported.
Fine-tuning dataset size, train/validation/test split, held-out evaluation design, and contamination controls are not reported.
There is a model-reporting inconsistency: Table 2 lists Qwen-2.5-3B-Instruct as the base model, while Figure 9 is captioned as QLoRA-finetuned Qwen3-4B-Instruct.
Maximum refinement-round budget R_max is referenced but not specified; the empirical sample size behind the reported Case 3 percentages is not reported.
Token budgets, context-window sizes, latency, cost, and inference compute are not reported for the multi-agent system or LLM judge.
Video-synthesis evaluation metrics, exact rendering platform/model/version, number of rendered videos, and selection protocol are not reported.
